rules.rule: If the lesion shows marked asymmetry in both shape and color distribution across two axes, it favors Melanoma.
If the border is irregular, notched, or has an abrupt cutoff with sharp angular indentations, it favors Melanoma.
If the lesion contains three or more distinct colors (e.g., tan, brown, dark brown, black, red, blue, white) in an irregular distribution, it favors Melanoma.
If a blue-white veil is present (a structureless blue-white area overlying a raised or irregular portion of the lesion), it favors Melanoma.
If regression structures are present — white scar-like areas or blue-gray peppering (multiple small blue-gray dots or granules) — it favors Melanoma.
If the pigment network is atypical — with irregular, thickened, or abruptly ending meshwork, or branched streaks radiating from the border — it favors Melanoma.
If dots and globules are distributed asymmetrically, especially clustered at the periphery or in an irregular pattern, it favors Melanoma.
If irregular vascular structures are visible — such as dotted vessels in an asymmetric pattern, milky-red areas, or polymorphous vessels — it favors Melanoma.
If the lesion is symmetric in both shape and color distribution, with smooth radial gradients, it favors Melanocytic Nevus.
If the border is regular and smooth, with a gradual transition from center to periphery, it favors Melanocytic Nevus.
If the color is uniform or shows only one to two shades of brown with even distribution, it favors Melanocytic Nevus.
If the pigment network is typical — a regular meshwork of thin lines that fades smoothly at the periphery — it favors Melanocytic Nevus.
If globules are present in a symmetric, central, or even distribution without peripheral clustering, it favors Melanocytic Nevus.

If no blue-white veil, no regression structures, no atypical network, no irregular dots at periphery, and no milky-red areas are visible, and the lesion has a symmetric shape with gradual color transition, the absence of all melanoma-specific structures argues for Melanocytic Nevus.

If no typical/regular pigment network is visible, the color distribution is not uniform, and the lesion lacks the symmetric fading at the periphery typical of a melanocytic nevus, the absence of nevus-typical organization argues for Melanoma.

If the overall dermoscopic pattern is organized and predictable — central darker area fading evenly to periphery, or peripheral network fading smoothly — even in the absence of a clearly typical pigment network, the overall organized pattern argues for a Melanocytic Nevus rather than Melanoma.
